meta.title: The Auction
meta.description: How bidding works: levels, suit ranking, the contract

blocks.content:
## The Auction: Bridge's Conversation

The auction is what makes bridge unique among card games. Before a single card is played, the four players engage in a structured conversation — using bids instead of words — to determine the contract. It's part negotiation, part information exchange, and part strategic battle.

Think of the auction as a promise. When your partnership wins the auction, you've promised to win a certain number of tricks. If you deliver on that promise, you score. If you don't, the other side scores. The auction determines those stakes.

## Bidding Basics

The dealer starts the auction, and it proceeds **clockwise**. On each turn, a player can:

- **Bid**: State a level (1 through 7) and a strain (♣, ♦, ♥, ♠, or NT)
- **Pass**: "I have nothing to say right now"
- **Double**: "I don't think you can make that" (only after an opponent's bid)
- **Redouble**: "Oh yes we can!" (only after an opponent's double)

## Understanding a Bid

Every bid has two parts:

1. **The level** (1-7): How many tricks beyond the book of six
2. **The strain** (♣, ♦, ♥, ♠, or NT): What the trump suit will be, or notrump

So a bid of **"2♥"** means: *"My partnership will try to win at least 8 tricks (2+6) with hearts as the trump suit."*

Here's what each level commits you to:

| Bid Level | Tricks Promised | Book + Level |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| 1         | 7              | 6 + 1        |
| 2         | 8              | 6 + 2        |
| 3         | 9              | 6 + 3        |
| 4         | 10             | 6 + 4        |
| 5         | 11             | 6 + 5        |
| 6         | 12             | 6 + 6        |
| 7         | 13             | 6 + 7        |

## The Five Strains

The "strain" is what determines the trump suit (or lack thereof). There are exactly five strains, ranked from lowest to highest:

| Rank | Strain | Name | Also Called |
|------|--------|------|-------------|
| 1 (lowest) | ♣ | Clubs | Minor |
| 2 | ♦ | Diamonds | Minor |
| 3 | ♥ | Hearts | Major |
| 4 | ♠ | Spades | Major |
| 5 (highest) | NT | Notrump | — |

The major suits (♥ and ♠) and notrump are ranked higher than the minor suits (♣ and ♦). As we'll see when we learn about scoring, this ranking reflects the fact that major-suit and notrump contracts are more valuable.

## Each Bid Must Be Higher

Here's the critical rule: **each new bid must be higher than the last one**. A bid is higher if:

- It's at a **higher level** (2-anything beats 1-anything), OR
- It's at the **same level** but a **higher-ranking strain**

So after a bid of 1♥, the legal bids are:
- **1♠** or **1NT** (same level, higher strain)
- **2♣** or **2♦** or **2♥** or **2♠** or **2NT** (higher level, any strain)
- And everything above that

After a bid of 1♥, you **cannot** bid 1♣ or 1♦ — those are lower strains at the same level.

This creates a natural ordering of all 35 possible bids, from lowest (1♣) to highest (7NT):

> 1♣ → 1♦ → 1♥ → 1♠ → 1NT → 2♣ → 2♦ → 2♥ → ... → 7♠ → 7NT

blocks.prompt: Which bid is higher: 2♥ or 1♠? Think about the two ways a bid can be higher than another.

blocks.content:
**2♥ is higher than 1♠.** Even though spades outrank hearts, the level matters first. Any 2-level bid is higher than any 1-level bid.

Here are a few more comparisons to build your intuition:

| Bid A | Bid B | Which is higher? | Why? |
|-------|-------|-----------------|------|
| 1♣ | 1♦ | 1♦ | Same level, ♦ outranks ♣ |
| 1♠ | 1NT | 1NT | Same level, NT outranks ♠ |
| 1NT | 2♣ | 2♣ | Higher level always wins |
| 3♦ | 3♣ | 3♦ | Same level, ♦ outranks ♣ |
| 2NT | 3♣ | 3♣ | Higher level always wins |
| 4♠ | 4NT | 4NT | Same level, NT outranks ♠ |

## How the Auction Ends

The auction continues clockwise until **three consecutive players pass**. The last bid before those three passes becomes the **contract**.

Here's a simple auction example:

| South | West | North | East |
|-------|------|-------|------|
| 1♥ | Pass | 2♥ | Pass |
| 4♥ | Pass | Pass | Pass |

South opens 1♥. West passes. North raises to 2♥ (supporting hearts). East passes. South bids 4♥ (going for game). Then three passes in a row — the auction is over. The contract is **4♥**.

What if nobody wants to bid at all?

| South | West | North | East |
|-------|------|-------|------|
| Pass | Pass | Pass | Pass |

Four passes means no one wanted to bid. The deal is **passed out** — no contract is made, no cards are played, and the deal moves to the next hand.

## Double and Redouble

Two special calls add drama to the auction:

**Double**: After an opponent bids, you can say "Double." This doesn't change the contract — it increases the stakes. If the declaring side makes the contract, they score more. If they go down, the penalties are steeper.

A double is often used when you believe the opponents have bid too high. The common phrase is "I don't think you can make that!"

**Redouble**: After an opponent doubles your side's bid, you can say "Redouble." This says "We're confident — raise the stakes even more!" It further increases both the rewards and penalties.

Important: Double and Redouble are **not bids** — they don't raise the level. After a double, someone can still bid normally at a higher level, which cancels the double.

## The Contract

The final bid in the auction becomes the contract. It specifies:

1. **The level**: How many tricks the declaring side must win (level + 6)
2. **The strain**: The trump suit, or notrump
3. **Doubled or redoubled?**: Whether the stakes have been raised

For example:
- **3NT** = Win 9+ tricks, notrump
- **4♠ Doubled** = Win 10+ tricks, spades are trumps, penalties and bonuses doubled
- **2♦ Redoubled** = Win 8+ tricks, diamonds are trumps, stakes quadrupled from base

## Why the Auction Matters

The auction isn't just a formality — it's half the game. Here's why:

- **Finding the right contract** means finding the right trump suit (or notrump) AND the right level. Bid too low and you miss bonus points. Bid too high and you go down.
- **Communication with partner**: Through your bids, you tell your partner about your hand's strength and shape. Your partner does the same. Together, you piece together a picture of your combined 26 cards.
- **Information for the defense**: The bidding also gives the defenders clues about where the cards are. Every bid reveals something.

Bridge bidding systems — the agreed-upon meanings behind each bid — are one of the deepest aspects of the game. We'll start learning these in Level 3.

problems.question: Which bid is higher: 1♠ or 2♣?
options: D) It depends on the situation
problems.explanation: 2♣ is higher because it's at a higher level. Any 2-level bid outranks any 1-level bid, regardless of the strain.

problems.question: After a bid of 2♦, which of these is the LOWEST legal bid?
problems.explanation: 2♥ is the lowest legal bid after 2♦. It's the same level but a higher-ranking strain (♥ outranks ♦). 2♣ is lower, 2♦ is equal (you can't rebid the same bid), and 3♣ is legal but not the lowest option.

problems.question: If the bidding goes 1♣ - 1♠ - 2♦ - ?, which of these can East legally bid?
problems.explanation: The last bid was 2♦, so East needs something higher. 2♥ (same level, higher strain) is the lowest legal bid. 2♣ and 1NT are too low, and 2♦ is the same as the last bid.

problems.question: How many tricks must the declaring side win if the contract is 5♦?
problems.explanation: The contract level plus the book: 5 + 6 = 11 tricks. The declaring side must win at least 11 of the 13 tricks.

problems.question: In the strain ranking, which suit is ranked immediately above diamonds?
problems.explanation: The strain ranking from low to high is: ♣ < ♦ < ♥ < ♠ < NT. Hearts (♥) is the next strain above diamonds (♦).

reviewQuestions.question: In the strain ranking, which is the highest?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Notrump ranks above all four suits. The order is ♣ < ♦ < ♥ < ♠ < NT.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: You got it! NT sits at the top of the strain ranking.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Close! Spades is the highest-ranking suit, but notrump (NT) ranks above even spades.

reviewQuestions.question: A bid of 3♣ promises to win how many tricks?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The bid level (3) plus the book (6) equals 9 tricks that the declaring side must win.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it! Always add 6 to the bid level to get the number of tricks needed.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Remember the book of 6: you always add 6 to the bid level. So 3 + 6 = 9 tricks.

reviewQuestions.question: What happens when four players pass at the start of the auction?
options: The deal is passed out with no play
The lowest possible contract (1♣) is used
reviewQuestions.explanation: If all four players pass, the deal is passed out -- no contract is established, no cards are played, and the hand is over.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! No bids means no contract, so the hand is simply passed out.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: When nobody bids, the deal is passed out. No contract is made, no cards are played, and the game moves to the next hand.
